## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md correctly describes the task: bring the running service into conformance with the Standard (body + Appendix G), fix the stalled audit ledger, keep existing routes, make schema migration idempotent, and avoid stale in-process state. The Standard itself is the normative spec and is available at /app/docs/standard.md. All behaviors verified by the tests (CORS allowlist, preflight, bootstrap evaluation order/status codes, health auth, token storage, audit schema/origin recording, history preservation, no debug disclosure) are derivable from the Standard as the instruction directs. The instruction explicitly calls out the long-context nature of the task and the need to reconcile against every applicable control.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests cover all normative controls and Appendix G amendments: CO-ORIGIN-ALLOW + G-2026-01 (two-origin allowlist, exact match, Vary header), CO-PREFLIGHT + G-2026-02 (204, Max-Age 300, scoped hints), AC-BOOTSTRAP + G-2026-03/05 (409 on repeat, precedence of already-bootstrapped over secret check), AC-TOKEN-STORE (mode 0600, non-recoverable storage), AC-HEALTH + G-2026-04 (401 for missing/wrong token, 'missing_credentials' reason), EH-NO-DISCLOSE (no X-Debug-Mode, no trace), AU-LEDGER-SCOPE + G-2026-06 (origin column, history preservation, idempotent migration), and a 45-case randomized lifecycle test against the hidden reference simulator.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a clear name describing the control being tested (e.g., test_bootstrap_already_bootstrapped_is_409, test_cors_exact_allowlist, test_token_stored_non_recoverable) and a docstring citing the specific control ID and amendment (e.g., 'G-2026-03: a repeat bootstrap is refused with 409 (not 403)'). The file is logically organized: module docstring explaining the two-layer approach, a scripted fixture for deterministic scripted tests, followed by individual targeted tests, then a randomized lifecycle test. Inline comments in helper functions also document which control applies.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: Neither solution/ nor tests/ is copied into the Docker image — the Dockerfile only COPYs harbordesk/ and docs/. The helpers.py reference implementation is noted as 'mounted at verification time only' and the test runner uses PYTHONPATH=/tests. The environment contains intentionally broken code (sticky CORS via static variables, stale bootstrap/token caching, wrong audit schema) that must be fixed by reading and understanding the Standard. The bootstrap_secret in the environment is a required operational value, not a shortcut to the answer. The 45-case randomized test with a fixed seed checks full lifecycle correctness against the hidden reference.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The audit ledger schema is normatively specified: AU-LEDGER-SCOPE in the Standard names the required fields (timestamp, event class, route, decision, reason, origin), G-2026-06 specifies migration away from the legacy 'actor' column to add 'origin', and the Dockerfile comment shows the legacy schema. The API's JSON response shapes are specified in the Standard controls (AC-BOOTSTRAP: {token: ...} for 201; AC-HEALTH: {status: 'ok'} for 200; EH-NO-DISCLOSE: {error: '...'} for errors). This is adequate — the schema is in the referenced spec file, not merely implied by examples.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: requirements.txt pins all Python/pip packages (iniconfig, packaging, pluggy, pytest, pytest-json-ctrf) with exact versions and SHA-256 hashes, using --require-hashes. The pip install uses --require-hashes and --no-deps. apt packages (php-cli, php-sqlite3, sqlite3, curl, etc.) are not pinned, which is acceptable per the stated policy.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names across any of the files examined. Paths like /app/harbordesk/data/admin_token, /app/harbordesk/data/audit.db, /app/harbordesk/data/bootstrap_secret are consistent throughout Dockerfile, helpers.py, config.php, and test.sh. PHP function names, SQL column names, and control IDs are consistent.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs harbordesk/ and docs/ into the image. Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory appears in any COPY instruction. The .dockerignore does not need to exclude them since they are never referenced by COPY.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh copies oracle PHP files (config.php, http.php, audit.php, index.php, start.sh) into /app/harbordesk/. These PHP files perform genuine computation: CORS logic iterates the allowlist and sets headers per request; the audit migration checks PRAGMA table_info and conditionally renames/recreates the table; bootstrap reads the secret from disk each request; health reads and hashes the token per request; token generation uses random_bytes. The solution demonstrates the steps an agent would perform (modify the PHP source files), not an echo of the final answer.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: The task does not require the agent to produce named output files for the tests to check. The agent must modify the running PHP service files in place. The relevant paths (/app/harbordesk/data/admin_token, /app/harbordesk/data/audit.db) are pre-existing infrastructure paths mentioned in the Standard and environment setup, not agent-produced output files.

Our HarborDesk Edge API in `/app/harbordesk` still reflects an older edition of
the HarborDesk Edge API Hardening Standard in `/app/docs/standard.md`. Operators
report it disagrees with policy on several fronts, and the SQLite audit ledger at
`/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db` has stopped taking new rows even though requests
still get answers. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4).

Bring the running service into conformance so that, for every request, the API
returns exactly the status, headers, and body the Standard as amended requires
and records exactly the audit row it implies. The stalled ledger and the
behaviors called out above are only the symptoms operators happened to notice;
reconcile the service against every applicable control in the Standard, not just
those. Keep the existing routes (`GET /health`, `POST /admin/bootstrap`) and
their JSON shapes. The ledger already
holds historical rows that reconciliation must preserve; note that the on-disk
ledger is restored to its older layout before every run, so the migration has to
be idempotent runtime code that reconciles the schema whenever the database is
opened (effectively on each request) — a one-off migration done once at deploy
time will be wiped and won't survive. Verification replays many request
lifecycles against one long-lived PHP process, so behavior must be correct per
request — cross-origin grants, bootstrap eligibility, and credential checks must
follow the current request and on-disk state, not stale in-process bookkeeping
left over from earlier requests in the same run.

PHP, SQLite, and `curl` are already installed and everything runs offline.

Agent removes static/sticky origin caching so CORS grants reflect the current request Origin header not stale state from prior requests, +3

Agent implements idempotent per-request schema migration that detects the legacy layout (actor column present or origin column missing) and reconciles to the new schema, +5

Agent adds the origin column to the audit_log schema so new audit rows record the request origin, +3

                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to reconcile a PHP-based HarborDesk Edge API with a
long security hardening standard document (plus authoritative Appendix G
amendments). The agent must fix CORS policy, authentication, bootstrap flow
ordering, debug disclosure, token storage, and a broken SQLite audit ledger
schema migration — all while preserving legacy data. The solution implements
comprehensive fixes across multiple PHP files. The test suite uses both
deterministic scripted replays and 45 randomized property-based lifecycle checks
against a hidden reference implementation.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Filename Overlap Between environment/ and solution/
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/harbordesk/ and tbench-task/solution/
Problem: Several filenames appear in both the environment and solution
         directories: index.php, config.php, audit.php, http.php, start.sh.
         While this is intentional for a "fix the broken code" task (the
         environment contains the buggy versions the agent must repair), it
         means the task relies on the agent NOT being able to see the solution/
         directory at runtime. This is fine by design — the harness mounts
         solution/ separately — but the high overlap warrants noting.

Current approach: Environment ships broken/misconfigured versions of the same
files that appear (corrected) in solution/.

Explanation: This is acceptable for a debug/fix task since the environment
versions are intentionally buggy (debug=true, allow_plaintext_bootstrap=true,
wrong CORS, broken audit schema migration, etc.). The agent cannot see
solution/ at runtime. No action required, but reviewers should confirm the
harness never exposes solution/ to the agent workspace.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Non-Canonical Base Image for a PHP Task
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 1)
Problem: The Dockerfile uses the Python 3.13 slim base image but the task is
         primarily a PHP task (languages = ["php", "bash", "sql"]). PHP is then
         installed via apt. While functional, this uses a Python base for a
         non-Python workload — the canonical base image list may not have a PHP
         entry yet, but this should be confirmed.

Current approach:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FROM public.ecr.aws/docker/library/python:3.13-slim-bookworm@sha256:...   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The Python base is needed because tests use pytest (Python), and
the task also needs PHP. Using the Python canonical image and installing
php-cli via apt is a pragmatic choice given that both runtimes are required.
This is acceptable but worth noting — a Debian slim base with both PHP and
Python installed might be more semantically correct if the canonical list
supports it.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Consider Adding Explicit pytest Version Pin Comment in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 20)
         tbench-task/environment/requirements.txt (line 17)

Current approach: Dependencies are properly pinned and hash-locked in
requirements.txt (pytest==8.2.2 with hashes). The Dockerfile uses
--require-hashes --no-deps for maximum reproducibility.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --require-hashes --no-deps \                │

[Container] 2026/06/27 03:44:58.895675 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our HarborDesk Edge API in `/app/harbordesk` still reflects an older edition of
the HarborDesk Edge API Hardening Standard in `/app/docs/standard.md`. Operators
report it disagrees with policy on several fronts, and the SQLite audit ledger at
`/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db` has stopped taking new rows even though requests
still get answers. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4).

Bring the running service into conformance so that, for every request, the API
returns exactly the status, headers, and body the Standard as amended requires
and records exactly the audit row it implies. The stalled ledger and the
behaviors called out above are only the symptoms operators happened to notice;
reconcile the service against every applicable control in the Standard, not just
those. Keep the existing routes (`GET /health`, `POST /admin/bootstrap`) and
their JSON shapes. The ledger already
holds historical rows that reconciliation must preserve; note that the on-disk
ledger is restored to its older layout before every run, so the migration has to
be idempotent runtime code that reconciles the schema whenever the database is
opened (effectively on each request) — a one-off migration done once at deploy
time will be wiped and won't survive. Verification replays many request
lifecycles against one long-lived PHP process, so behavior must be correct per
request — cross-origin grants, bootstrap eligibility, and credential checks must
follow the current request and on-disk state, not stale in-process bookkeeping
left over from earlier requests in the same run.

PHP, SQLite, and `curl` are already installed and everything runs offline.

2026-06-27 03:46:09,587 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (21 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite combines targeted deterministic assertions for each control
with a 45-case randomized property test that replays full request lifecycles
against a hidden reference implementation, making shortcuts infeasible.

Strengths:  Excellent layered testing strategy — deterministic tests pin each
individual control and Appendix G amendment, while the randomized lifecycle
test covers combinatorial interactions (origin × auth × bootstrap state)
that scripted tests alone would miss. The reference simulator in helpers.py
faithfully encodes the resolved policy, and the test harness reseeds the
legacy ledger between cases to verify idempotent runtime migration.

Weaknesses: None significant. Minor observation that the Allow-Headers value
is only tested on the preflight path (not explicitly confirmed absent on
disallowed-origin preflights in a dedicated assertion), but the randomized
test covers this implicitly via _assert_response's CORS block.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite verifies every core constraint from the HarborDesk Edge API
Hardening Standard as amended by Appendix G. It tests HTTP status codes,
response headers (CORS grants, Vary, debug suppression), JSON body content,
token storage security (file mode and non-recoverability), audit ledger
correctness (event, route, origin, decision, reason), and legacy history
preservation. The 45 randomized lifecycle cases with a fixed seed ensure
deterministic reproducibility while covering diverse request orderings that
would be impractical to script by hand. The reference implementation is
judge-only (in tests/helpers.py), the bootstrap secret is read at runtime
from the data directory, and no shortcut exists that could pass all tests
without genuinely implementing the resolved policy.

================================================================================

output_data.formatted_report:
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite combines targeted deterministic assertions for each control
with a 45-case randomized property test that replays full request lifecycles
against a hidden reference implementation, making shortcuts infeasible.

Strengths:  Excellent layered testing strategy — deterministic tests pin each
individual control and Appendix G amendment, while the randomized lifecycle
test covers combinatorial interactions (origin × auth × bootstrap state)
that scripted tests alone would miss. The reference simulator in helpers.py
faithfully encodes the resolved policy, and the test harness reseeds the
legacy ledger between cases to verify idempotent runtime migration.

Weaknesses: None significant. Minor observation that the Allow-Headers value
is only tested on the preflight path (not explicitly confirmed absent on
disallowed-origin preflights in a dedicated assertion), but the randomized
test covers this implicitly via _assert_response's CORS block.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite verifies every core constraint from the HarborDesk Edge API
Hardening Standard as amended by Appendix G. It tests HTTP status codes,
response headers (CORS grants, Vary, debug suppression), JSON body content,
token storage security (file mode and non-recoverability), audit ledger
correctness (event, route, origin, decision, reason), and legacy history
preservation. The 45 randomized lifecycle cases with a fixed seed ensure
deterministic reproducibility while covering diverse request orderings that
would be impractical to script by hand. The reference implementation is
judge-only (in tests/helpers.py), the bootstrap secret is read at runtime
from the data directory, and no shortcut exists that could pass all tests
without genuinely implementing the resolved policy.

================================================================================

[Container] 2026/06/27 03:44:58.895675 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our HarborDesk Edge API in `/app/harbordesk` still reflects an older edition of
the HarborDesk Edge API Hardening Standard in `/app/docs/standard.md`. Operators
report it disagrees with policy on several fronts, and the SQLite audit ledger at
`/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db` has stopped taking new rows even though requests
still get answers. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4).

Bring the running service into conformance so that, for every request, the API
returns exactly the status, headers, and body the Standard as amended requires
and records exactly the audit row it implies. The stalled ledger and the
behaviors called out above are only the symptoms operators happened to notice;
reconcile the service against every applicable control in the Standard, not just
those. Keep the existing routes (`GET /health`, `POST /admin/bootstrap`) and
their JSON shapes. The ledger already
holds historical rows that reconciliation must preserve; note that the on-disk
ledger is restored to its older layout before every run, so the migration has to
be idempotent runtime code that reconciles the schema whenever the database is
opened (effectively on each request) — a one-off migration done once at deploy
time will be wiped and won't survive. Verification replays many request
lifecycles against one long-lived PHP process, so behavior must be correct per
request — cross-origin grants, bootstrap eligibility, and credential checks must
follow the current request and on-disk state, not stale in-process bookkeeping
left over from earlier requests in the same run.

PHP, SQLite, and `curl` are already installed and everything runs offline.

✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md correctly describes the task: bring the running service into conformance with the Standard (body + Appendix G), fix the stalled audit ledger, keep existing routes, make schema migration idempotent, and avoid stale in-process state. All behaviors verified by the tests (CORS allowlist, preflight, bootstrap evaluation order/status codes, health auth, token storage, audit schema/origin recording, history preservation, no debug disclosure) are derivable from the Standard as the instruction directs. The instruction explicitly calls out the long-context nature of the task and the need to reconcile against every applicable control.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests cover all normative controls and Appendix G amendments: CO-ORIGIN-ALLOW + G-2026-01 (two-origin allowlist, exact match, Vary header), CO-PREFLIGHT + G-2026-02 (204, Max-Age 300, scoped hints), AC-BOOTSTRAP + G-2026-03/05 (409 on repeat, precedence of already-bootstrapped over secret check), AC-TOKEN-STORE (mode 0600, non-recoverable storage), AC-HEALTH + G-2026-04 (401 for missing/wrong token, 'missing_credentials' reason), EH-NO-DISCLOSE (no X-Debug-Mode, no trace), AU-LEDGER-SCOPE + G-2026-06 (origin column, history preservation, idempotent migration), and a 45-case randomized lifecycle test against the hidden reference simulator.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a clear name describing the control being tested (e.g., test_bootstrap_already_bootstrapped_is_409, test_cors_exact_allowlist, test_token_stored_non_recoverable) and a docstring citing the specific control ID and amendment (e.g., 'G-2026-03: a repeat bootstrap is refused with 409 (not 403)'). The file is logically organized: module docstring explaining the two-layer approach, a scripted fixture for deterministic scripted tests, followed by individual targeted tests, then a randomized lifecycle test. Inline comments in helper functions also document which control applies.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: Neither solution/ nor tests/ is copied into the Docker image — the Dockerfile only COPYs harbordesk/ and docs/. The helpers.py reference implementation is noted as 'mounted at verification time only' and the test runner uses PYTHONPATH=/tests. The environment contains intentionally broken code (sticky CORS via static variables, stale bootstrap/token caching, wrong audit schema) that must be fixed by reading and understanding the Standard. The bootstrap_secret in the environment is a required operational value, not a shortcut to the answer. The 45-case randomized test with a fixed seed checks full lifecycle correctness against the hidden reference.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The audit ledger schema is normatively specified: AU-LEDGER-SCOPE in the Standard names the required fields (timestamp, event class, route, decision, reason, origin), G-2026-06 specifies migration away from the legacy 'actor' column to add 'origin', and the Dockerfile comment shows the legacy schema. The API's JSON response shapes are specified in the Standard controls (AC-BOOTSTRAP: {token: ...} for 201; AC-HEALTH: {status: 'ok'} for 200; EH-NO-DISCLOSE: {error: '...'} for errors). This is adequate — the schema is in the referenced spec file, not merely implied by examples.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: requirements.txt pins all Python/pip packages (iniconfig, packaging, pluggy, pytest, pytest-json-ctrf) with exact versions and SHA-256 hashes, using --require-hashes. The pip install uses --require-hashes and --no-deps. apt packages (php-cli, php-sqlite3, sqlite3, curl, etc.) are not pinned, which is acceptable per the stated policy.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names across any of the files examined. Paths like /app/harbordesk/data/admin_token, /app/harbordesk/data/audit.db, /app/harbordesk/data/bootstrap_secret are consistent throughout Dockerfile, helpers.py, config.php, and test.sh. PHP function names, SQL column names, and control IDs are consistent.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs harbordesk/ and docs/ into the image. Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory appears in any COPY instruction. The .dockerignore does not need to exclude them since they are never referenced by COPY.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh copies oracle PHP files (config.php, http.php, audit.php, index.php, start.sh) into /app/harbordesk/. These PHP files perform genuine computation: CORS logic iterates the allowlist and sets headers per request; the audit migration checks PRAGMA table_info and conditionally renames/recreates the table; bootstrap reads the secret from disk each request; health reads and hashes the token per request; token generation uses random_bytes. The solution demonstrates the steps an agent would perform (modify the PHP source files), not an echo of the final answer.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: The task does not require the agent to produce named output files for the tests to check. The agent must modify the running PHP service files in place. The relevant paths (/app/harbordesk/data/admin_token, /app/harbordesk/data/audit.db) are pre-existing infrastructure paths mentioned in the Standard and environment setup, not agent-produced output files.

PHP function names, SQL column names, and control IDs are consistent.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs harbordesk/ and docs/ into the image. Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory appears in any COPY instruction. The .dockerignore does not need to exclude them since they are never referenced by COPY.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh copies oracle PHP files (config.php, http.php, audit.php, index.php, start.sh) into /app/harbordesk/. These PHP files perform genuine computation: CORS logic iterates the allowlist and sets headers per request; the audit migration checks PRAGMA table_info and conditionally renames/recreates the table; bootstrap reads the secret from disk each request; health reads and hashes the token per request; token generation uses random_bytes. The solution demonstrates the steps an agent would perform (modify the PHP source files), not an echo of the final answer.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: The task does not require the agent to produce named output files for the tests to check. The agent must modify the running PHP service files in place. The relevant paths (/app/harbordesk/data/admin_token, /app/harbordesk/data/audit.db) are pre-existing infrastructure paths mentioned in the Standard and environment setup, not agent-produced output files.

  • test_cors_rejects_untrusted_and_inexact: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_preflight_scoped_and_max_age: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_no_debug_details_leak: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_health_missing_reason_renamed: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_audit_history_preserved: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_audit_records_origin: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_randomized_lifecycles_match_reference: 8 passed / 10 runs

Analysis on Agent Failures:
  • Task Instruction Sufficiency: ❌ FAIL, ## Job Summary: HarborDesk Security Hardening (3 Trials)

### 1. Overall Results
**0/3 trials passed** (all-or-nothing grading: any failing test → score 0.0). Despite zero rewards, agent capability varied substantially:

| Trial | Tests Passed | Score |
|---|---|---|
| tbench-task__T8Nxxj6 | 2/16 | 0.0 |
| tbench-task__VHgTjoG | 12/16 | 0.0 |
| tbench-task__BS2wp79 | 14/16 | 0.0 |

### 2. Common Failure Patterns

**AC-TOKEN-STORE was missed by every agent that reached it.** Two requirements within this single control sank two of the three trials:
- **Token file mode 0600** (not 0644) — missed by BS2wp79
- **Token stored as a non-recoverable hash** (never cleartext) — missed by VHgTjoG and BS2wp79

Both agents that got this far did grep-based extraction of the standard and their output was truncated before reaching the AC-TOKEN-STORE section (around line 224), so they never saw the hashing requirement. This is a systematic context/truncation failure when reading a large policy document.

**Secondary miss — Amendment G-2026-03 (409 on re-bootstrap):** VHgTjoG returned HTTP 403 instead of 409 for already-bootstrapped requests, likely because the amendment appeared late in the document and scrolled off terminal output during a long-context read. BS2wp79 correctly caught this one.

### 3. Hack Check
**No cheating detected across all three trials.** All reward_hacking checks passed. Agents only read source files and the standard document, made legitimate PHP edits, and did not touch test files, `/logs/verifier/reward.txt`, `reward.json`, or the `solution/` directory.

### 4. Systematic Instruction Issues

Mild under-specification flagged in **BS2wp79** (`task_specification: fail`): The instruction focuses attention on "symptoms operators noticed" and the audit ledger stall, without enumerating all applicable controls. While it does say "reconcile against *every* applicable control," this relies entirely on the agent discovering AC-TOKEN-STORE by reading the full standard — which two agents failed to do due to output truncation. The other two trials passed `task_specification`, so this is a borderline finding. Recommendation: either enumerate the key controls explicitly in the instruction, or break the standard into smaller readable chunks to avoid truncation-driven blind spots.

**T8Nxxj6** failed catastrophically due to an infrastructure issue (`OutputLengthExceededError` → `AgentTimeoutError`) before any code changes were made — not an instruction problem.

### 5. Progress (Failed Trials)

| Trial | Root Cause of Failure | Proximity to Success |
|---|---|---|
| T8Nxxj6 | Token-limit timeout at step 3; no code changes | Very far (2/16) |
| VHgTjoG | Missed G-2026-03 (409) + token hashing | Close (12/16, 4 tests away) |
| BS2wp79 | Missed token file mode + token hashing | Very close (14/16, 2 tests away) |

Average meaningful progress: ~8/16 tests across all trials, or ~13/16 excluding the infrastructure failure.

### 6. Key Observations

All three trials used the same task; no model/agent variation was recorded in the summaries. The performance gap between T8Nxxj6 and the others is entirely attributable to the infrastructure crash. Among the two substantive attempts, BS2wp79 was the stronger run — it correctly implemented G-2026-03 (409 re-bootstrap) and the audit schema migration, leaving only the two AC-TOKEN-STORE sub-requirements unsolved. The single most impactful fix for future runs would be ensuring the agent fully reads and indexes the standard (especially lines ~220–235 covering AC-TOKEN-STORE) before writing any code.

Our HarborDesk Edge API in `/app/harbordesk` still reflects an older edition of
the HarborDesk Edge API Hardening Standard in `/app/docs/standard.md`. Operators
report it disagrees with policy on several fronts, and the SQLite audit ledger at
`/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db` has stopped taking new rows even though requests
still get answers. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4).

Bring the running service into conformance so that, for every request, the API
returns exactly the status, headers, and body the Standard as amended requires
and records exactly the audit row it implies. The stalled ledger and the
behaviors called out above are only the symptoms operators happened to notice;
reconcile the service against every applicable control in the Standard, not just
those. Keep the existing routes (`GET /health`, `POST /admin/bootstrap`) and
their JSON shapes. The ledger already
holds historical rows that reconciliation must preserve; note that the on-disk
ledger is restored to its older layout before every run, so the migration has to
be idempotent runtime code that reconciles the schema whenever the database is
opened (effectively on each request) — a one-off migration done once at deploy
time will be wiped and won't survive. Verification replays many request
lifecycles against one long-lived PHP process, so behavior must be correct per
request — cross-origin grants, bootstrap eligibility, and credential checks must
follow the current request and on-disk state, not stale in-process bookkeeping
left over from earlier requests in the same run.

PHP, SQLite, and `curl` are already installed and everything runs offline.

Agent reads and understands the Hardening Standard document including Appendix G amendments before making changes, +2
Agent introduces a configuration change that breaks an existing working route or removes a required config key, -3

# Rubric 2
Agent ensures Access-Control-Allow-Origin is echoed only for allowlisted origins (never wildcard '*'), +3
Agent emits Vary: Origin header when an allowed origin receives a CORS grant, +2
Agent restricts preflight hints (Allow-Methods, Allow-Headers, Max-Age) to allowed-origin requests only — disallowed origins receive no CORS headers at all, +3
Agent removes static/sticky origin caching so CORS grants reflect the current request Origin header not stale state from prior requests, +3
Agent sets Access-Control-Allow-Credentials true only for allowlisted origins, +2
Agent emits CORS grant headers for disallowed or absent origins, -5

# Rubric 3
Agent implements idempotent per-request schema migration that detects the legacy layout (actor column present or origin column missing) and reconciles to the new schema, +5
Agent preserves all pre-existing historical rows during the migration (copies them forward rather than dropping them), +5
Agent adds the origin column to the audit_log schema so new audit rows record the request origin, +3
Agent ensures audit writes do not silently fail — errors are surfaced or the insert reliably succeeds after migration, +2
Agent performs a one-off migration at deploy time that will be wiped on subsequent runs instead of making it idempotent runtime code, -5
Agent drops or truncates the audit_log table destroying historical rows, -5

# Rubric 4
Agent changes bootstrap already-bootstrapped refusal from 403 to 409 per amendment G-2026-03, +3
Agent reorders bootstrap evaluation so already-bootstrapped check comes before secret validation per amendment G-2026-05, +3
Agent stores the administrative token as a non-recoverable digest (e.g. SHA-256 hash) rather than plaintext per AC-TOKEN-STORE, +3
Agent sets token file permissions to 0600 (owner read/write only) per AC-TOKEN-STORE, +2
Agent uses constant-time comparison (hash_equals) for bootstrap secret validation, +2
Agent removes the per-request freshness of token-existence check (uses stale cached state for bootstrap eligibility), -3

Agent changes the missing-credential denial reason from missing_token to missing_credentials per amendment G-2026-04, +3
Agent verifies the presented bearer token by hashing it and comparing the digest against the stored representation (compatible with AC-TOKEN-STORE), +3
Agent uses constant-time comparison (hash_equals) for token verification in the health endpoint, +2
Agent configures start.sh to route all requests through the front controller (php -S ... index.php) so the router handles every path, +2
Agent verifies the service works by testing health and bootstrap endpoints after making changes, +2
Agent leaves the health endpoint accepting raw plaintext token comparison against the stored digest without hashing the presented token first, -3

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to reconcile a PHP-based HarborDesk Edge API with a
long security hardening standard document (plus authoritative Appendix G
amendments). The agent must fix CORS policy, authentication, bootstrap flow
ordering, debug disclosure, token storage, and a broken SQLite audit ledger
schema migration — all while preserving legacy data. The solution implements
comprehensive fixes across multiple PHP files. The test suite uses both
deterministic scripted replays and 45 randomized property-based lifecycle checks
against a hidden reference implementation.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Filename Overlap Between environment/ and solution/
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/harbordesk/ and tbench-task/solution/
Problem: Several filenames appear in both the environment and solution
         directories: index.php, config.php, audit.php, http.php, start.sh.
         While this is intentional for a "fix the broken code" task (the
         environment contains the buggy versions the agent must repair), it
         means the task relies on the agent NOT being able to see the solution/
         directory at runtime. This is fine by design — the harness mounts
         solution/ separately — but the high overlap warrants noting.

Current approach: Environment ships broken/misconfigured versions of the same
files that appear (corrected) in solution/.

Explanation: This is acceptable for a debug/fix task since the environment
versions are intentionally buggy (debug=true, allow_plaintext_bootstrap=true,
wrong CORS, broken audit schema migration, etc.). The agent cannot see
solution/ at runtime. No action required, but reviewers should confirm the
harness never exposes solution/ to the agent workspace.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Non-Canonical Base Image for a PHP Task
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 1)
Problem: The Dockerfile uses the Python 3.13 slim base image but the task is
         primarily a PHP task (languages = ["php", "bash", "sql"]). PHP is then
         installed via apt. While functional, this uses a Python base for a
         non-Python workload — the canonical base image list may not have a PHP
         entry yet, but this should be confirmed.

Current approach:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FROM public.ecr.aws/docker/library/python:3.13-slim-bookworm@sha256:...   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The Python base is needed because tests use pytest (Python), and
the task also needs PHP. Using the Python canonical image and installing
php-cli via apt is a pragmatic choice given that both runtimes are required.
This is acceptable but worth noting — a Debian slim base with both PHP and
Python installed might be more semantically correct if the canonical list
supports it.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Consider Adding Explicit pytest Version Pin Comment in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 20)
         tbench-task/environment/requirements.txt (line 17)

Current approach: Dependencies are properly pinned and hash-locked in
requirements.txt (pytest==8.2.2 with hashes). The Dockerfile uses
--require-hashes --no-deps for maximum reproducibility.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --require-hashes --no-deps \                │
│      -r /tmp/requirements.txt                                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: This is exemplary dependency management — hash-locked with no
transitive dependency surprises. No change needed; noted as a strength.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted, high-quality security task that tests an agent's
ability to reconcile a running service against a dense compliance document with
amendments. The long-context standard, subtle bugs, and randomized property
tests make cheating extremely difficult.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent anti-cheating design: randomized property tests with a hidden
    reference implementation make shortcutting impossible
  ✓ Hash-locked, fully-pinned dependencies with offline-compatible test.sh
  ✓ Realistic task structure — agent must read a long standard, understand
    amendment precedence, and fix multiple interacting bugs
  ✓ Comprehensive test coverage with both deterministic and property-based
    verification (45 randomized lifecycles)

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Minor: uses Python base image for a primarily PHP workload (pragmatic
    choice given pytest requirement, but slightly unconventional)

Evaluates: Long-context comprehension, security policy implementation,
           PHP/SQLite debugging, schema migration design

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  The task is well-structured, thoroughly tested, and properly configured for
  offline execution. No critical issues found. The warnings are informational
  and do not require changes before deployment.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Filename Overlap Between environment/ and solution/
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/harbordesk/ and tbench-task/solution/
Problem: Several filenames appear in both the environment and solution
         directories: index.php, config.php, audit.php, http.php, start.sh.
         While this is intentional for a "fix the broken code" task (the
         environment contains the buggy versions the agent must repair), it
         means the task relies on the agent NOT being able to see the solution/
         directory at runtime. This is fine by design — the harness mounts
         solution/ separately — but the high overlap warrants noting.

Current approach: Environment ships broken/misconfigured versions of the same
files that appear (corrected) in solution/.

Explanation: This is acceptable for a debug/fix task since the environment
versions are intentionally buggy (debug=true, allow_plaintext_bootstrap=true,
wrong CORS, broken audit schema migration, etc.). The agent cannot see
solution/ at runtime. No action required, but reviewers should confirm the
harness never exposes solution/ to the agent workspace.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Non-Canonical Base Image for a PHP Task
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 1)
Problem: The Dockerfile uses the Python 3.13 slim base image but the task is
         primarily a PHP task (languages = ["php", "bash", "sql"]). PHP is then
         installed via apt. While functional, this uses a Python base for a
         non-Python workload — the canonical base image list may not have a PHP
         entry yet, but this should be confirmed.

Current approach:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FROM public.ecr.aws/docker/library/python:3.13-slim-bookworm@sha256:...   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The Python base is needed because tests use pytest (Python), and
the task also needs PHP. Using the Python canonical image and installing
php-cli via apt is a pragmatic choice given that both runtimes are required.
This is acceptable but worth noting — a Debian slim base with both PHP and
Python installed might be more semantically correct if the canonical list
supports it.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Consider Adding Explicit pytest Version Pin Comment in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 20)
         tbench-task/environment/requirements.txt (line 17)

Current approach: Dependencies are properly pinned and hash-locked in
requirements.txt (pytest==8.2.2 with hashes). The Dockerfile uses
--require-hashes --no-deps for maximum reproducibility.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --require-hashes --no-deps \                │
│      -r /tmp/requirements.txt                                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: This is exemplary dependency management — hash-locked with no
transitive dependency surprises. No change needed; noted as a strength.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted, high-quality security task that tests an agent's
ability to reconcile a running service against a dense compliance document with
amendments. The long-context standard, subtle bugs, and randomized property
tests make cheating extremely difficult.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent anti-cheating design: randomized property tests with a hidden
    reference implementation make shortcutting impossible
  ✓ Hash-locked, fully-pinned dependencies with offline-compatible test.sh
  ✓ Realistic task structure — agent must read a long standard, understand
    amendment precedence, and fix multiple interacting bugs
  ✓ Comprehensive test coverage with both deterministic and property-based
    verification (45 randomized lifecycles)

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Minor: uses Python base image for a primarily PHP workload (pragmatic
    choice given pytest requirement, but slightly unconventional)

Evaluates: Long-context comprehension, security policy implementation,
           PHP/SQLite debugging, schema migration design

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  The task is well-structured, thoroughly tested, and properly configured for
  offline execution. No critical issues found. The warnings are informational
  and do not require changes before deployment.
================================================================================
output_data.rubric:
# Rubric 1
Agent disables debug mode (sets debug to false in config.php) so no X-Debug-Mode header or trace field is emitted, +3
Agent disables allow_plaintext_bootstrap so bootstrap requires the secret header, +3
Agent adds https://ops.harbordesk.internal to allowed_origins per amendment G-2026-01, +2
Agent sets preflight_max_age to 300 per amendment G-2026-02, +2
Agent reads and understands the Hardening Standard document including Appendix G amendments before making changes, +2
Agent introduces a configuration change that breaks an existing working route or removes a required config key, -3

# Rubric 2
Agent ensures Access-Control-Allow-Origin is echoed only for allowlisted origins (never wildcard '*'), +3
Agent emits Vary: Origin header when an allowed origin receives a CORS grant, +2
Agent restricts preflight hints (Allow-Methods, Allow-Headers, Max-Age) to allowed-origin requests only — disallowed origins receive no CORS headers at all, +3
Agent removes static/sticky origin caching so CORS grants reflect the current request Origin header not stale state from prior requests, +3
Agent sets Access-Control-Allow-Credentials true only for allowlisted origins, +2
Agent emits CORS grant headers for disallowed or absent origins, -5

# Rubric 3
Agent implements idempotent per-request schema migration that detects the legacy layout (actor column present or origin column missing) and reconciles to the new schema, +5
Agent preserves all pre-existing historical rows during the migration (copies them forward rather than dropping them), +5
Agent adds the origin column to the audit_log schema so new audit rows record the request origin, +3
Agent ensures audit writes do not silently fail — errors are surfaced or the insert reliably succeeds after migration, +2
Agent performs a one-off migration at deploy time that will be wiped on subsequent runs instead of making it idempotent runtime code, -5
Agent drops or truncates the audit_log table destroying historical rows, -5

# Rubric 4
Agent changes bootstrap already-bootstrapped refusal from 403 to 409 per amendment G-2026-03, +3
Agent reorders bootstrap evaluation so already-bootstrapped check comes before secret validation per amendment G-2026-05, +3
Agent stores the administrative token as a non-recoverable digest (e.g. SHA-256 hash) rather than plaintext per AC-TOKEN-STORE, +3
Agent sets token file permissions to 0600 (owner read/write only) per AC-TOKEN-STORE, +2
Agent uses constant-time comparison (hash_equals) for bootstrap secret validation, +2
Agent removes the per-request freshness of token-existence check (uses stale cached state for bootstrap eligibility), -3

# Rubric 5
Agent changes the missing-credential denial reason from missing_token to missing_credentials per amendment G-2026-04, +3
Agent verifies the presented bearer token by hashing it and comparing the digest against the stored representation (compatible with AC-TOKEN-STORE), +3
Agent uses constant-time comparison (hash_equals) for token verification in the health endpoint, +2
Agent configures start.sh to route all requests through the front controller (php -S ... index.php) so the router handles every path, +2
Agent verifies the service works by testing health and bootstrap endpoints after making changes, +2
Agent leaves the health endpoint accepting raw plaintext token comparison against the stored digest without hashing the presented token first, -3

Our HarborDesk Edge API in `/app/harbordesk` still reflects an older edition of
the HarborDesk Edge API Hardening Standard in `/app/docs/standard.md`. Operators
report it disagrees with policy on several fronts, and the SQLite audit ledger at
`/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db` has stopped taking new rows even though requests
still get answers. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4).

Bring the running service into conformance so that, for every request, the API
returns exactly the status, headers, and body the Standard as amended requires
and records exactly the audit row it implies. The stalled ledger and the
behaviors called out above are only the symptoms operators happened to notice;
reconcile the service against every applicable control in the Standard, not just
those. Keep the existing routes (`GET /health`, `POST /admin/bootstrap`) and
their JSON shapes. The ledger already
holds historical rows that reconciliation must preserve; note that the on-disk
ledger is restored to its older layout before every run, so the migration has to
be idempotent runtime code that reconciles the schema whenever the database is
opened (effectively on each request) — a one-off migration done once at deploy
time will be wiped and won't survive. Verification replays many request
lifecycles against one long-lived PHP process, so behavior must be correct per
request — cross-origin grants, bootstrap eligibility, and credential checks must
follow the current request and on-disk state, not stale in-process bookkeeping
left over from earlier requests in the same run.

PHP, SQLite, and `curl` are already installed and everything runs offline.

# Rubric 1
Agent disables debug mode (sets debug to false in config.php) so no X-Debug-Mode header or trace field is emitted, +3
Agent disables allow_plaintext_bootstrap so bootstrap requires the secret header, +3
Agent adds https://ops.harbordesk.internal to allowed_origins per amendment G-2026-01, +2
Agent sets preflight_max_age to 300 per amendment G-2026-02, +2
Agent reads and understands the Hardening Standard document including Appendix G amendments before making changes, +2
Agent introduces a configuration change that breaks an existing working route or removes a required config key, -3

# Rubric 2
Agent ensures Access-Control-Allow-Origin is echoed only for allowlisted origins (never wildcard '*'), +3
Agent emits Vary: Origin header when an allowed origin receives a CORS grant, +2
Agent restricts preflight hints (Allow-Methods, Allow-Headers, Max-Age) to allowed-origin requests only — disallowed origins receive no CORS headers at all, +3
Agent removes static/sticky origin caching so CORS grants reflect the current request Origin header not stale state from prior requests, +3
Agent sets Access-Control-Allow-Credentials true only for allowlisted origins, +2
Agent emits CORS grant headers for disallowed or absent origins, -5

# Rubric 3
Agent implements idempotent per-request schema migration that detects the legacy layout (actor column present or origin column missing) and reconciles to the new schema, +5
Agent preserves all pre-existing historical rows during the migration (copies them forward rather than dropping them), +5
Agent adds the origin column to the audit_log schema so new audit rows record the request origin, +3
Agent ensures audit writes do not silently fail — errors are surfaced or the insert reliably succeeds after migration, +2
Agent performs a one-off migration at deploy time that will be wiped on subsequent runs instead of making it idempotent runtime code, -5
Agent drops or truncates the audit_log table destroying historical rows, -5

# Rubric 4
Agent changes bootstrap already-bootstrapped refusal from 403 to 409 per amendment G-2026-03, +3
Agent reorders bootstrap evaluation so already-bootstrapped check comes before secret validation per amendment G-2026-05, +3
Agent stores the administrative token as a non-recoverable digest (e.g. SHA-256 hash) rather than plaintext per AC-TOKEN-STORE, +3
Agent sets token file permissions to 0600 (owner read/write only) per AC-TOKEN-STORE, +2
Agent uses constant-time comparison (hash_equals) for bootstrap secret validation, +2
Agent removes the per-request freshness of token-existence check (uses stale cached state for bootstrap eligibility), -3

# Rubric 5
Agent changes the missing-credential denial reason from missing_token to missing_credentials per amendment G-2026-04, +3
Agent verifies the presented bearer token by hashing it and comparing the digest against the stored representation (compatible with AC-TOKEN-STORE), +3
Agent uses constant-time comparison (hash_equals) for token verification in the health endpoint, +2
Agent configures start.sh to route all requests through the front controller (php -S ... index.php) so the router handles every path, +2
Agent verifies the service works by testing health and bootstrap endpoints after making changes, +2
Agent leaves the health endpoint accepting raw plaintext token comparison against the stored digest without hashing the presented token first, -3
Reviewing task: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task (skill: terminus-2nd-ed-task-reviewer)
Review output: ================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to reconcile a PHP-based HarborDesk Edge API with a
long security hardening standard document (plus authoritative Appendix G
amendments). The agent must fix CORS policy, authentication, bootstrap flow
ordering, debug disclosure, token storage, and a broken SQLite audit ledger
schema migration — all while preserving legacy data. The solution implements
comprehensive fixes across multiple PHP files. The test suite uses both
deterministic scripted replays and 45 randomized property-based lifecycle checks
against a hidden reference implementation.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Filename Overlap Between environment/ and solution/
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/harbordesk/ and tbench-task/solution/
Problem: Several filenames appear in both the environment and solution
         directories: index.php, config.php, audit.php, http.php, start.sh.
         While this is intentional for a "fix the broken code" task (the
         environment contains the buggy versions the agent must repair), it
         means the task relies on the agent NOT being able to see the solution/
         directory at runtime. This is fine by design — the harness mounts
         solution/ separately — but the high overlap warrants noting.

Current approach: Environment ships broken/misconfigured versions of the same
files that appear (corrected) in solution/.

Explanation: This is acceptable for a debug/fix task since the environment
versions are intentionally buggy (debug=true, allow_plaintext_bootstrap=true,
wrong CORS, broken audit schema migration, etc.). The agent cannot see
solution/ at runtime. No action required, but reviewers should confirm the
harness never exposes solution/ to the agent workspace.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Non-Canonical Base Image for a PHP Task
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 1)
Problem: The Dockerfile uses the Python 3.13 slim base image but the task is
         primarily a PHP task (languages = ["php", "bash", "sql"]). PHP is then
         installed via apt. While functional, this uses a Python base for a
         non-Python workload — the canonical base image list may not have a PHP
         entry yet, but this should be confirmed.

Current approach:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FROM public.ecr.aws/docker/library/python:3.13-slim-bookworm@sha256:...   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The Python base is needed because tests use pytest (Python), and
the task also needs PHP. Using the Python canonical image and installing
php-cli via apt is a pragmatic choice given that both runtimes are required.
This is acceptable but worth noting — a Debian slim base with both PHP and
Python installed might be more semantically correct if the canonical list
supports it.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Consider Adding Explicit pytest Version Pin Comment in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 20)
         tbench-task/environment/requirements.txt (line 17)

Current approach: Dependencies are properly pinned and hash-locked in
requirements.txt (pytest==8.2.2 with hashes). The Dockerfile uses
--require-hashes --no-deps for maximum reproducibility.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --require-hashes --no-deps \                │
│      -r /tmp/requirements.txt                                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: This is exemplary dependency management — hash-locked with no
transitive dependency surprises. No change needed; noted as a strength.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted, high-quality security task that tests an agent's
ability to reconcile a running service against a dense compliance document with
amendments. The long-context standard, subtle bugs, and randomized property
tests make cheating extremely difficult.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent anti-cheating design: randomized property tests with a hidden
    reference implementation make shortcutting impossible
  ✓ Hash-locked, fully-pinned dependencies with offline-compatible test.sh
  ✓ Realistic task structure — agent must read a long standard, understand
    amendment precedence, and fix multiple interacting bugs
  ✓ Comprehensive test coverage with both deterministic and property-based
    verification (45 randomized lifecycles)

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Minor: uses Python base image for a primarily PHP workload (pragmatic
    choice given pytest requirement, but slightly unconventional)

Evaluates: Long-context comprehension, security policy implementation,
           PHP/SQLite debugging, schema migration design

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  The task is well-structured, thoroughly tested, and properly configured for
  offline execution. No critical issues found. The warnings are informational
  and do not require changes before deployment.
================================================================================

[Container] 2026/06/27 13:13:32.089366 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our HarborDesk Edge API in `/app/harbordesk` still reflects an older edition of
the HarborDesk Edge API Hardening Standard in `/app/docs/standard.md`. Operators
report it disagrees with policy on several fronts, and the SQLite audit ledger at
`/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db` has stopped taking new rows even though requests
still get answers. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4).

Bring the running service into conformance so that, for every request, the API
returns exactly the status, headers, and body the Standard as amended requires
and records exactly the audit row it implies. Reconcile the full set of controls
the tests exercise: exact-origin CORS and preflight behavior, bootstrap secret
and already-bootstrapped ordering, AC-TOKEN-STORE token handling (the raw bearer
token is returned once but must not be persisted in recoverable form, and the
token file is owner-only), authenticated health checks and denial reasons,
debug disclosure rules, and audit logging.

Keep the existing routes (`GET /health`, `POST /admin/bootstrap`) and their JSON
shapes. The ledger already holds historical rows that reconciliation must
preserve; note that the on-disk ledger is restored to its older layout before
every run, so the migration has to be idempotent runtime code that reconciles
the schema whenever the database is opened (effectively on each request) — a
one-off migration done once at deploy time will be wiped and won't survive.
Verification replays many request lifecycles against one long-lived PHP process,
so behavior must be correct per request — cross-origin grants, bootstrap
eligibility, and credential checks must follow the current request and on-disk
state, not stale in-process bookkeeping left over from earlier requests in the
same run.

PHP, SQLite, and `curl` are already installed and everything runs offline.

2026-06-27 13:14:21,355 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (21 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite provides thorough correctness verification through both
deterministic pinning tests and a randomized property-based harness that
replays 45 varied request lifecycles against a comprehensive reference
implementation.

Strengths:  The combination of targeted deterministic tests for each control
(CORS, bootstrap, health, audit, token storage, debug disclosure) with a
randomized lifecycle test that exercises all interactions in varied order
creates strong coverage. The reference implementation in helpers.py faithfully
encodes the resolved policy from the Standard as amended by Appendix G, and
the agent cannot access test files.

Weaknesses: The standard document is very large (~2800 lines) with many
filler controls, which could confuse an agent, but the test suite itself is
well-designed and not exploitable.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite verifies every core constraint from the instruction through
two complementary layers. First, 15 deterministic tests pin individual
controls: bootstrap ordering (G-2026-03, G-2026-05), CORS exact-match
allowlist (G-2026-01), preflight max-age (G-2026-02), token file security
(AC-TOKEN-STORE mode 0600 and non-recoverability), health authentication
(AC-HEALTH), audit reason renaming (G-2026-04), origin recording (G-2026-06),
legacy row preservation (AU-LEDGER-SCOPE), and debug suppression
(EH-NO-DISCLOSE). Second, a seeded randomized test replays 45 complete
request lifecycles against the reference simulator, asserting exact status
codes, CORS headers, body content, and audit ledger row-by-row correctness
including origin. The test.sh reseeds the legacy audit database before each
run, ensuring the schema migration must be idempotent runtime code. No
shortcut solution exists: the agent must fix multiple interacting bugs in
the PHP application (CORS logic, bootstrap ordering, token hashing, file
permissions, debug suppression, audit schema migration, reason renaming)
across several files, all validated by the hidden reference oracle.

================================================================================

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md describes all behaviors that the tests exercise: exact-origin CORS and preflight behavior, bootstrap secret and already-bootstrapped ordering, AC-TOKEN-STORE token handling (raw bearer token returned once, not persisted in recoverable form, file is owner-only), authenticated health checks and denial reasons, debug disclosure rules (EH-NO-DISCLOSE), and audit logging. It explicitly names the routes to keep (GET /health, POST /admin/bootstrap), mentions the JSON shapes must be preserved, explains idempotent runtime schema migration, and warns that behavior must follow per-request state (not stale in-process bookkeeping). The instruction also explains that the Standard plus Appendix G are the authoritative source of behavior and that where body and amendment conflict, the amendment governs.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests cover all behaviors described in instruction.md: CORS exact-origin matching (test_cors_exact_allowlist, test_cors_rejects_untrusted_and_inexact), preflight behavior with Max-Age (test_preflight_scoped_and_max_age), bootstrap secret validation (test_bootstrap_requires_secret), already-bootstrapped 409 response (test_bootstrap_already_bootstrapped_is_409), G-2026-05 ordering (test_bootstrap_already_precedes_secret_check), token file permissions (test_token_file_not_world_readable), non-recoverable token storage (test_token_stored_non_recoverable), health check auth (test_health_requires_valid_token), debug disclosure (test_no_debug_details_leak), audit logging with origin (test_audit_records_origin), legacy history preservation (test_audit_history_preserved), renamed denial reason (test_health_missing_reason_renamed), and 45 randomized lifecycles against the reference. The Vary header requirement and stale-process-state bugs are also covered via the randomized tests and deterministic fixture.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Tests are well-organized: there is a session-scoped 'scripted' fixture that runs a deterministic request sequence once, and individual tests each have a clear docstring naming the control or amendment being checked (e.g. 'G-2026-03: a repeat bootstrap is refused with 409', 'AC-TOKEN-STORE: the raw bearer token is not recoverable from the file'). The randomized lifecycle test is clearly separated and documented. Helper functions in helpers.py are also well-commented.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The helpers.py reference implementation (the ground truth) is mounted only at verification time from tests/ which is not copied into the Docker image. The .dockerignore does not explicitly exclude tests/ or solution/, but the Dockerfile only COPYs harbordesk/ and docs/ into the image — it does not COPY tests/ or solution/. The bootstrap secret is a static file in data/, not embedded in instruction.md. The task intentionally plants several bugs (sticky CORS via static variables, stale in-process token/bootstrap caching, audit schema mismatch, wrong HTTP status for repeated bootstrap, wrong denial reason name, debug disclosure enabled, wildcard CORS origin, missing origin in allowlist, missing Vary header) that must each be independently diagnosed and fixed; there is no trivially copyable answer in the environment.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The task does not require the agent to produce a structured output file. The API response schemas (JSON bodies with 'status', 'token', 'error' fields) and the audit ledger schema (columns: id, ts, event, route, origin, decision, reason) are described normatively within the Standard (standard.md) and implicitly through instruction.md's references to the Standard. The instruction explicitly tells agents to 'Keep the existing routes and their JSON shapes.' The audit schema migration requirements are clearly explained in instruction.md. No additional schema documentation is needed.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: requirements.txt uses --require-hashes and --no-deps with fully pinned versions for all Python packages (iniconfig==2.3.0, packaging==26.2, pluggy==1.6.0, pytest==8.2.2, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5), each with multiple hash entries. The Dockerfile uses a pinned Python base image digest (@sha256:01f42367...). The apt packages (php-cli, php-sqlite3, sqlite3, curl, etc.) are intentionally not pinned, which is acceptable per the evaluation criteria.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. File paths like /app/harbordesk/data/admin_token, /app/harbordesk/data/audit.db, /app/docs/standard.md are consistent throughout Dockerfile, helpers.py, config.php, and test files. Control names (AC-TOKEN-STORE, AC-BOOTSTRAP, AC-HEALTH, CO-ORIGIN-ALLOW, CO-PREFLIGHT, EH-NO-DISCLOSE, AU-LEDGER-SCOPE) are used consistently. Amendment references (G-2026-01 through G-2026-06) are consistently named.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs 'harbordesk/' and 'docs/' into the image (the COPY directives are 'COPY harbordesk/ /app/harbordesk/' and 'COPY docs/ /app/docs/'). Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory is copied into the Docker image. The test runner mounts tests/ at verification time via the PYTHONPATH=/tests reference in test.sh.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: The solution (solve.sh) does not hardcode or echo any final answers. It simply copies the corrected PHP files (config.php, http.php, audit.php, index.php, start.sh) from the solution/ directory to the app directory. These PHP files implement the required behavior through actual logic: CORS header generation via allowlist lookup, audit schema migration via PRAGMA table_info + ALTER TABLE + INSERT SELECT, token storage via hash('sha256', $token), file permission setting via chmod, bootstrap-order enforcement via is_file checks, etc. The solution demonstrates the steps an agent would perform.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: The task does not ask the agent to produce any output files that tests check for by filename. The agent modifies existing PHP source files in /app/harbordesk/ (which are already present in the image), and the tests check the running API's HTTP responses and the SQLite audit ledger at a fixed path (/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db). The audit.db path is mentioned in instruction.md as '/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db'. No new output files need to be created by the agent.

Our HarborDesk Edge API in `/app/harbordesk` still reflects an older edition of
the HarborDesk Edge API Hardening Standard in `/app/docs/standard.md`. Operators
report it disagrees with policy on several fronts, and the SQLite audit ledger at
`/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db` has stopped taking new rows even though requests
still get answers. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4).

Bring the running service into conformance so that, for every request, the API
returns exactly the status, headers, and body the Standard as amended requires
and records exactly the audit row it implies. Reconcile the full set of controls
the tests exercise: exact-origin CORS and preflight behavior, bootstrap secret
and already-bootstrapped ordering, AC-TOKEN-STORE token handling (the raw bearer
token is returned once but must not be persisted in recoverable form, and the
token file is owner-only), authenticated health checks and denial reasons,
debug disclosure rules, and audit logging.

Keep the existing routes (`GET /health`, `POST /admin/bootstrap`) and their JSON
shapes. The ledger already holds historical rows that reconciliation must
preserve; note that the on-disk ledger is restored to its older layout before
every run, so the migration has to be idempotent runtime code that reconciles
the schema whenever the database is opened (effectively on each request) — a
one-off migration done once at deploy time will be wiped and won't survive.
Verification replays many request lifecycles against one long-lived PHP process,
so behavior must be correct per request — cross-origin grants, bootstrap
eligibility, and credential checks must follow the current request and on-disk
state, not stale in-process bookkeeping left over from earlier requests in the
same run.

PHP, SQLite, and `curl` are already installed and everything runs offline.
