2026-06-27 13:14:21,355 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (21 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite provides thorough correctness verification through both
deterministic pinning tests and a randomized property-based harness that
replays 45 varied request lifecycles against a comprehensive reference
implementation.

Strengths:  The combination of targeted deterministic tests for each control
(CORS, bootstrap, health, audit, token storage, debug disclosure) with a
randomized lifecycle test that exercises all interactions in varied order
creates strong coverage. The reference implementation in helpers.py faithfully
encodes the resolved policy from the Standard as amended by Appendix G, and
the agent cannot access test files.

Weaknesses: The standard document is very large (~2800 lines) with many
filler controls, which could confuse an agent, but the test suite itself is
well-designed and not exploitable.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite verifies every core constraint from the instruction through
two complementary layers. First, 15 deterministic tests pin individual
controls: bootstrap ordering (G-2026-03, G-2026-05), CORS exact-match
allowlist (G-2026-01), preflight max-age (G-2026-02), token file security
(AC-TOKEN-STORE mode 0600 and non-recoverability), health authentication
(AC-HEALTH), audit reason renaming (G-2026-04), origin recording (G-2026-06),
legacy row preservation (AU-LEDGER-SCOPE), and debug suppression
(EH-NO-DISCLOSE). Second, a seeded randomized test replays 45 complete
request lifecycles against the reference simulator, asserting exact status
codes, CORS headers, body content, and audit ledger row-by-row correctness
including origin. The test.sh reseeds the legacy audit database before each
run, ensuring the schema migration must be idempotent runtime code. No
shortcut solution exists: the agent must fix multiple interacting bugs in
the PHP application (CORS logic, bootstrap ordering, token hashing, file

output_data.formatted_report:
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite provides thorough correctness verification through both
deterministic pinning tests and a randomized property-based harness that
replays 45 varied request lifecycles against a comprehensive reference
implementation.

Strengths:  The combination of targeted deterministic tests for each control
(CORS, bootstrap, health, audit, token storage, debug disclosure) with a
randomized lifecycle test that exercises all interactions in varied order
creates strong coverage. The reference implementation in helpers.py faithfully
encodes the resolved policy from the Standard as amended by Appendix G, and
the agent cannot access test files.

Weaknesses: The standard document is very large (~2800 lines) with many
filler controls, which could confuse an agent, but the test suite itself is
well-designed and not exploitable.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite verifies every core constraint from the instruction through
two complementary layers. First, 15 deterministic tests pin individual
controls: bootstrap ordering (G-2026-03, G-2026-05), CORS exact-match
allowlist (G-2026-01), preflight max-age (G-2026-02), token file security
(AC-TOKEN-STORE mode 0600 and non-recoverability), health authentication
(AC-HEALTH), audit reason renaming (G-2026-04), origin recording (G-2026-06),
legacy row preservation (AU-LEDGER-SCOPE), and debug suppression
(EH-NO-DISCLOSE). Second, a seeded randomized test replays 45 complete
request lifecycles against the reference simulator, asserting exact status
codes, CORS headers, body content, and audit ledger row-by-row correctness
including origin. The test.sh reseeds the legacy audit database before each
run, ensuring the schema migration must be idempotent runtime code. No
shortcut solution exists: the agent must fix multiple interacting bugs in
the PHP application (CORS logic, bootstrap ordering, token hashing, file
permissions, debug suppression, audit schema migration, reason renaming)
across several files, all validated by the hidden reference oracle.

================================================================================

[Container] 2026/06/27 13:13:32.089366 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our HarborDesk Edge API in `/app/harbordesk` still reflects an older edition of
the HarborDesk Edge API Hardening Standard in `/app/docs/standard.md`. Operators
report it disagrees with policy on several fronts, and the SQLite audit ledger at
`/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db` has stopped taking new rows even though requests
still get answers. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4).

Bring the running service into conformance so that, for every request, the API
returns exactly the status, headers, and body the Standard as amended requires
and records exactly the audit row it implies. Reconcile the full set of controls
the tests exercise: exact-origin CORS and preflight behavior, bootstrap secret
and already-bootstrapped ordering, AC-TOKEN-STORE token handling (the raw bearer
token is returned once but must not be persisted in recoverable form, and the
token file is owner-only), authenticated health checks and denial reasons,
debug disclosure rules, and audit logging.

Keep the existing routes (`GET /health`, `POST /admin/bootstrap`) and their JSON
shapes. The ledger already holds historical rows that reconciliation must
preserve; note that the on-disk ledger is restored to its older layout before
every run, so the migration has to be idempotent runtime code that reconciles
the schema whenever the database is opened (effectively on each request) — a
one-off migration done once at deploy time will be wiped and won't survive.
Verification replays many request lifecycles against one long-lived PHP process,
so behavior must be correct per request — cross-origin grants, bootstrap
eligibility, and credential checks must follow the current request and on-disk
state, not stale in-process bookkeeping left over from earlier requests in the
same run.

PHP, SQLite, and `curl` are already installed and everything runs offline.

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md describes all behaviors that the tests exercise: exact-origin CORS and preflight behavior, bootstrap secret and already-bootstrapped ordering, AC-TOKEN-STORE token handling (raw bearer token returned once, not persisted in recoverable form, file is owner-only), authenticated health checks and denial reasons, debug disclosure rules (EH-NO-DISCLOSE), and audit logging. It explicitly names the routes to keep (GET /health, POST /admin/bootstrap), mentions the JSON shapes must be preserved, explains idempotent runtime schema migration, and warns that behavior must follow per-request state (not stale in-process bookkeeping). The instruction also explains that the Standard plus Appendix G are the authoritative source of behavior and that where body and amendment conflict, the amendment governs.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests cover all behaviors described in instruction.md: CORS exact-origin matching (test_cors_exact_allowlist, test_cors_rejects_untrusted_and_inexact), preflight behavior with Max-Age (test_preflight_scoped_and_max_age), bootstrap secret validation (test_bootstrap_requires_secret), already-bootstrapped 409 response (test_bootstrap_already_bootstrapped_is_409), G-2026-05 ordering (test_bootstrap_already_precedes_secret_check), token file permissions (test_token_file_not_world_readable), non-recoverable token storage (test_token_stored_non_recoverable), health check auth (test_health_requires_valid_token), debug disclosure (test_no_debug_details_leak), audit logging with origin (test_audit_records_origin), legacy history preservation (test_audit_history_preserved), renamed denial reason (test_health_missing_reason_renamed), and 45 randomized lifecycles against the reference. The Vary header requirement and stale-process-state bugs are also covered via the randomized tests and deterministic fixture.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Tests are well-organized: there is a session-scoped 'scripted' fixture that runs a deterministic request sequence once, and individual tests each have a clear docstring naming the control or amendment being checked (e.g. 'G-2026-03: a repeat bootstrap is refused with 409', 'AC-TOKEN-STORE: the raw bearer token is not recoverable from the file'). The randomized lifecycle test is clearly separated and documented. Helper functions in helpers.py are also well-commented.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The helpers.py reference implementation (the ground truth) is mounted only at verification time from tests/ which is not copied into the Docker image. The .dockerignore does not explicitly exclude tests/ or solution/, but the Dockerfile only COPYs harbordesk/ and docs/ into the image — it does not COPY tests/ or solution/. The bootstrap secret is a static file in data/, not embedded in instruction.md. The task intentionally plants several bugs (sticky CORS via static variables, stale in-process token/bootstrap caching, audit schema mismatch, wrong HTTP status for repeated bootstrap, wrong denial reason name, debug disclosure enabled, wildcard CORS origin, missing origin in allowlist, missing Vary header) that must each be independently diagnosed and fixed; there is no trivially copyable answer in the environment.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The task does not require the agent to produce a structured output file. The API response schemas (JSON bodies with 'status', 'token', 'error' fields) and the audit ledger schema (columns: id, ts, event, route, origin, decision, reason) are described normatively within the Standard (standard.md) and implicitly through instruction.md's references to the Standard. The instruction explicitly tells agents to 'Keep the existing routes and their JSON shapes.' The audit schema migration requirements are clearly explained in instruction.md. No additional schema documentation is needed.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: requirements.txt uses --require-hashes and --no-deps with fully pinned versions for all Python packages (iniconfig==2.3.0, packaging==26.2, pluggy==1.6.0, pytest==8.2.2, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5), each with multiple hash entries. The Dockerfile uses a pinned Python base image digest (@sha256:01f42367...). The apt packages (php-cli, php-sqlite3, sqlite3, curl, etc.) are intentionally not pinned, which is acceptable per the evaluation criteria.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. File paths like /app/harbordesk/data/admin_token, /app/harbordesk/data/audit.db, /app/docs/standard.md are consistent throughout Dockerfile, helpers.py, config.php, and test files. Control names (AC-TOKEN-STORE, AC-BOOTSTRAP, AC-HEALTH, CO-ORIGIN-ALLOW, CO-PREFLIGHT, EH-NO-DISCLOSE, AU-LEDGER-SCOPE) are used consistently. Amendment references (G-2026-01 through G-2026-06) are consistently named.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs 'harbordesk/' and 'docs/' into the image (the COPY directives are 'COPY harbordesk/ /app/harbordesk/' and 'COPY docs/ /app/docs/'). Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory is copied into the Docker image. The test runner mounts tests/ at verification time via the PYTHONPATH=/tests reference in test.sh.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: The solution (solve.sh) does not hardcode or echo any final answers. It simply copies the corrected PHP files (config.php, http.php, audit.php, index.php, start.sh) from the solution/ directory to the app directory. These PHP files implement the required behavior through actual logic: CORS header generation via allowlist lookup, audit schema migration via PRAGMA table_info + ALTER TABLE + INSERT SELECT, token storage via hash('sha256', $token), file permission setting via chmod, bootstrap-order enforcement via is_file checks, etc. The solution demonstrates the steps an agent would perform.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: The task does not ask the agent to produce any output files that tests check for by filename. The agent modifies existing PHP source files in /app/harbordesk/ (which are already present in the image), and the tests check the running API's HTTP responses and the SQLite audit ledger at a fixed path (/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db). The audit.db path is mentioned in instruction.md as '/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db'. No new output files need to be created by the agent.

output_data.quality_check_summary:
## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md describes all behaviors that the tests exercise: exact-origin CORS and preflight behavior, bootstrap secret and already-bootstrapped ordering, AC-TOKEN-STORE token handling (raw bearer token returned once, not persisted in recoverable form, file is owner-only), authenticated health checks and denial reasons, debug disclosure rules (EH-NO-DISCLOSE), and audit logging. It explicitly names the routes to keep (GET /health, POST /admin/bootstrap), mentions the JSON shapes must be preserved, explains idempotent runtime schema migration, and warns that behavior must follow per-request state (not stale in-process bookkeeping). The instruction also explains that the Standard plus Appendix G are the authoritative source of behavior and that where body and amendment conflict, the amendment governs.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests cover all behaviors described in instruction.md: CORS exact-origin matching (test_cors_exact_allowlist, test_cors_rejects_untrusted_and_inexact), preflight behavior with Max-Age (test_preflight_scoped_and_max_age), bootstrap secret validation (test_bootstrap_requires_secret), already-bootstrapped 409 response (test_bootstrap_already_bootstrapped_is_409), G-2026-05 ordering (test_bootstrap_already_precedes_secret_check), token file permissions (test_token_file_not_world_readable), non-recoverable token storage (test_token_stored_non_recoverable), health check auth (test_health_requires_valid_token), debug disclosure (test_no_debug_details_leak), audit logging with origin (test_audit_records_origin), legacy history preservation (test_audit_history_preserved), renamed denial reason (test_health_missing_reason_renamed), and 45 randomized lifecycles against the reference. The Vary header requirement and stale-process-state bugs are also covered via the randomized tests and deterministic fixture.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Tests are well-organized: there is a session-scoped 'scripted' fixture that runs a deterministic request sequence once, and individual tests each have a clear docstring naming the control or amendment being checked (e.g. 'G-2026-03: a repeat bootstrap is refused with 409', 'AC-TOKEN-STORE: the raw bearer token is not recoverable from the file'). The randomized lifecycle test is clearly separated and documented. Helper functions in helpers.py are also well-commented.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The helpers.py reference implementation (the ground truth) is mounted only at verification time from tests/ which is not copied into the Docker image. The .dockerignore does not explicitly exclude tests/ or solution/, but the Dockerfile only COPYs harbordesk/ and docs/ into the image — it does not COPY tests/ or solution/. The bootstrap secret is a static file in data/, not embedded in instruction.md. The task intentionally plants several bugs (sticky CORS via static variables, stale in-process token/bootstrap caching, audit schema mismatch, wrong HTTP status for repeated bootstrap, wrong denial reason name, debug disclosure enabled, wildcard CORS origin, missing origin in allowlist, missing Vary header) that must each be independently diagnosed and fixed; there is no trivially copyable answer in the environment.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The task does not require the agent to produce a structured output file. The API response schemas (JSON bodies with 'status', 'token', 'error' fields) and the audit ledger schema (columns: id, ts, event, route, origin, decision, reason) are described normatively within the Standard (standard.md) and implicitly through instruction.md's references to the Standard. The instruction explicitly tells agents to 'Keep the existing routes and their JSON shapes.' The audit schema migration requirements are clearly explained in instruction.md. No additional schema documentation is needed.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: requirements.txt uses --require-hashes and --no-deps with fully pinned versions for all Python packages (iniconfig==2.3.0, packaging==26.2, pluggy==1.6.0, pytest==8.2.2, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5), each with multiple hash entries. The Dockerfile uses a pinned Python base image digest (@sha256:01f42367...). The apt packages (php-cli, php-sqlite3, sqlite3, curl, etc.) are intentionally not pinned, which is acceptable per the evaluation criteria.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. File paths like /app/harbordesk/data/admin_token, /app/harbordesk/data/audit.db, /app/docs/standard.md are consistent throughout Dockerfile, helpers.py, config.php, and test files. Control names (AC-TOKEN-STORE, AC-BOOTSTRAP, AC-HEALTH, CO-ORIGIN-ALLOW, CO-PREFLIGHT, EH-NO-DISCLOSE, AU-LEDGER-SCOPE) are used consistently. Amendment references (G-2026-01 through G-2026-06) are consistently named.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs 'harbordesk/' and 'docs/' into the image (the COPY directives are 'COPY harbordesk/ /app/harbordesk/' and 'COPY docs/ /app/docs/'). Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory is copied into the Docker image. The test runner mounts tests/ at verification time via the PYTHONPATH=/tests reference in test.sh.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: The solution (solve.sh) does not hardcode or echo any final answers. It simply copies the corrected PHP files (config.php, http.php, audit.php, index.php, start.sh) from the solution/ directory to the app directory. These PHP files implement the required behavior through actual logic: CORS header generation via allowlist lookup, audit schema migration via PRAGMA table_info + ALTER TABLE + INSERT SELECT, token storage via hash('sha256', $token), file permission setting via chmod, bootstrap-order enforcement via is_file checks, etc. The solution demonstrates the steps an agent would perform.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: The task does not ask the agent to produce any output files that tests check for by filename. The agent modifies existing PHP source files in /app/harbordesk/ (which are already present in the image), and the tests check the running API's HTTP responses and the SQLite audit ledger at a fixed path (/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db). The audit.db path is mentioned in instruction.md as '/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db'. No new output files need to be created by the agent.

It explicitly names the routes to keep (GET /health, POST /admin/bootstrap), mentions the JSON shapes must be preserved, explains idempotent runtime schema migration, and warns that behavior must follow per-request state (not stale in-process bookkeeping). The instruction also explains that the Standard plus Appendix G are the authoritative source of behavior and that where body and amendment conflict, the amendment governs.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests cover all behaviors described in instruction.md: CORS exact-origin matching (test_cors_exact_allowlist, test_cors_rejects_untrusted_and_inexact), preflight behavior with Max-Age (test_preflight_scoped_and_max_age), bootstrap secret validation (test_bootstrap_requires_secret), already-bootstrapped 409 response (test_bootstrap_already_bootstrapped_is_409), G-2026-05 ordering (test_bootstrap_already_precedes_secret_check), token file permissions (test_token_file_not_world_readable), non-recoverable token storage (test_token_stored_non_recoverable), health check auth (test_health_requires_valid_token), debug disclosure (test_no_debug_details_leak), audit logging with origin (test_audit_records_origin), legacy history preservation (test_audit_history_preserved), renamed denial reason (test_health_missing_reason_renamed), and 45 randomized lifecycles against the reference. The Vary header requirement and stale-process-state bugs are also covered via the randomized tests and deterministic fixture.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Tests are well-organized: there is a session-scoped 'scripted' fixture that runs a deterministic request sequence once, and individual tests each have a clear docstring naming the control or amendment being checked (e.g. 'G-2026-03: a repeat bootstrap is refused with 409', 'AC-TOKEN-STORE: the raw bearer token is not recoverable from the file'). The randomized lifecycle test is clearly separated and documented. Helper functions in helpers.py are also well-commented.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The helpers.py reference implementation (the ground truth) is mounted only at verification time from tests/ which is not copied into the Docker image. The .dockerignore does not explicitly exclude tests/ or solution/, but the Dockerfile only COPYs harbordesk/ and docs/ into the image — it does not COPY tests/ or solution/. The bootstrap secret is a static file in data/, not embedded in instruction.md. The task intentionally plants several bugs (sticky CORS via static variables, stale in-process token/bootstrap caching, audit schema mismatch, wrong HTTP status for repeated bootstrap, wrong denial reason name, debug disclosure enabled, wildcard CORS origin, missing origin in allowlist, missing Vary header) that must each be independently diagnosed and fixed; there is no trivially copyable answer in the environment.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The task does not require the agent to produce a structured output file. ✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. Control names (AC-TOKEN-STORE, AC-BOOTSTRAP, AC-HEALTH, CO-ORIGIN-ALLOW, CO-PREFLIGHT, EH-NO-DISCLOSE, AU-LEDGER-SCOPE) are used consistently. Amendment references (G-2026-01 through G-2026-06) are consistently named.

Difficulty: ✅ MEDIUM

Status: ✅ Solvable (all tests passed by at least one agent run)

Agent Performance:
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-8: 80.0% (4/5 runs)
  • terminus-gpt5-5: 60.0% (3/5 runs)

Reference Agents:
  • nop: 0.0% (0/1 runs)
  • oracle: 100.0% (3/3 runs)

Failure Breakdown:
  • nop: 1 other
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-8: 1 other
  • terminus-gpt5-5: 2 other

Unit Tests Results:
  • test_bootstrap_already_bootstrapped_is_409: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_cors_rejects_untrusted_and_inexact: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_audit_history_preserved: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_routing_health_reachable: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_bootstrap_requires_secret: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_bootstrap_succeeds_with_secret: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_bootstrap_already_precedes_secret_check: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_token_file_not_world_readable: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_token_stored_non_recoverable: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_health_requires_valid_token: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_cors_exact_allowlist: 7 passed / 10 runs
  • test_preflight_scoped_and_max_age: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_no_debug_details_leak: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_health_missing_reason_renamed: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_audit_records_origin: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_randomized_lifecycles_match_reference: 7 passed / 10 runs

Analysis on Agent Failures:
  • Task Instruction Sufficiency: ❌ FAIL, ## Job Summary

### 1. Overall Results
**0 of 3 trials passed** (0% success rate). All three trials — `tbench-task__VtVvD9K`, `tbench-task__LohN7t2`, and `tbench-task__ncBzx4c` — resulted in a reward of 0. No agent/model distinction is noted across trials; they appear to use the same or similar model configurations.

---

### 2. Common Failure Patterns

**Primary failure (2/3 trials): Missing `Vary: Origin` header**
Trials `VtVvD9K` and `ncBzx4c` both passed 14/16 tests and failed on the exact same two tests:
- `test_cors_exact_allowlist`
- `test_randomized_lifecycles_match_reference`

Both agents implemented CORS correctly in most respects but omitted the `Vary: Origin` header from `apply_cors()` in `lib/http.php`. This requirement is **explicitly stated** in the standard at CO-ORIGIN-ALLOW section 712. It is a single-line oversight that caused a complete 0 reward — suggesting the grader uses all-or-nothing scoring.

**Secondary failure (1/3 trials): Broad misreading of requirements**
Trial `LohN7t2` was far worse, passing only 3/16 tests. The agent made multiple independent errors:
- Wrong CORS origin domains (`ops.harbordesk.example` vs. the correct `harbordesk.internal` / `ops.harbordesk.internal`)
- Wrong HTTP status codes (401 instead of 403 for bootstrap failures; 200 instead of 401 for unauthenticated `/health`)
- Missing `origin` column in the audit schema
- Incorrect ordering of malformed-JSON vs. already-bootstrapped checks

---

### 3. Hack Check ✅
**No cheating detected.** All three trials received a `reward_hacking: pass`. Every agent solved the task through legitimate means — reading source files, rewriting PHP application code, and running local `curl`-based smoke tests. No agent modified test files, accessed the `solution/` directory, or wrote to reward files.

---

### 4. Systematic Instruction Issues
The `task_specification` check flags a **real problem in trial `LohN7t2`** (`task_specification: fail`). Key specification gaps:
- **CORS origins** are never listed in `instruction.md`; the correct values only appear in `helpers.py`, which the agent may not have accessed.
- **HTTP status codes** for bootstrap errors are ambiguous given amendment-ordering in the standard.
- **`/health` behavior** (200 vs. 401) is contradictory between the instruction's mention of "liveness probes" and the test's 401 expectation.
- The **`origin` audit column** requirement is never stated in `instruction.md`.

Trials `VtVvD9K` and `ncBzx4c` received `task_specification: pass` — their failure was purely an implementation oversight, not an instruction gap.

---

### 5. Progress on Failed Trials

| Trial | Tests Passed | Root Cause |
|---|---|---|
| `VtVvD9K` | 14/16 (87.5%) | Single missing `Vary: Origin` header |
| `ncBzx4c` | 14/16 (87.5%) | Same: single missing `Vary: Origin` header |
| `LohN7t2` | 3/16 (18.75%) | Multiple misread requirements, likely compounded by spec ambiguity |

Two trials were **extremely close** to success (one header away). The third was a more fundamental failure.

---

### 6. Key Takeaways & Recommendations

- **Fix the `Vary: Origin` gap**: Two of the three agents independently missed the same single requirement. Consider either adding a more prominent callout in the instruction, or investigating whether agents are systematically skipping certain subsections of the standard document.
- **Improve instruction completeness**: The `LohN7t2` failure is partly attributable to spec gaps (CORS origins, status codes, audit schema). Making `helpers.py` accessible or mirroring its key values in `instruction.md` would reduce ambiguity.
- **All-or-nothing scoring is unforgiving**: With 14/16 correct, two trials scored 0 identically to a 3/16 trial. If partial credit were available, `VtVvD9K` and `ncBzx4c` would have been near-passing runs.

Our HarborDesk Edge API in `/app/harbordesk` still reflects an older edition of
the HarborDesk Edge API Hardening Standard in `/app/docs/standard.md`. Operators
report it disagrees with policy on several fronts, and the SQLite audit ledger at
`/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db` has stopped taking new rows even though requests
still get answers. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4).

Bring the running service into conformance so that, for every request, the API
returns exactly the status, headers, and body the Standard as amended requires
and records exactly the audit row it implies. Reconcile the full set of controls
the tests exercise: exact-origin CORS and preflight behavior, bootstrap secret
and already-bootstrapped ordering, AC-TOKEN-STORE token handling (the raw bearer
token is returned once but must not be persisted in recoverable form, and the
token file is owner-only), authenticated health checks and denial reasons,
debug disclosure rules, and audit logging.

Keep the existing routes (`GET /health`, `POST /admin/bootstrap`) and their JSON
shapes. The ledger already holds historical rows that reconciliation must
preserve; note that the on-disk ledger is restored to its older layout before
every run, so the migration has to be idempotent runtime code that reconciles
the schema whenever the database is opened (effectively on each request) — a
one-off migration done once at deploy time will be wiped and won't survive.
Verification replays many request lifecycles against one long-lived PHP process,
so behavior must be correct per request — cross-origin grants, bootstrap
eligibility, and credential checks must follow the current request and on-disk
state, not stale in-process bookkeeping left over from earlier requests in the
same run.

PHP, SQLite, and `curl` are already installed and everything runs offline.

python3 /app/scripts/skills/terminal_bench_rubric_generator/rubric-claude-single.py "harbor_tasks/${TASK}" --output output/rubric-claude-single.txt --output-json output/rubric.json --milestone

# Run review generation script
python3 /app/scripts/skills/terminal_bench_2_task_reviewer/review-claude-single.py "harbor_tasks/${TASK}" --output output/review-claude-single.txt --output-json output/review.json --skill terminus-2nd-ed-task-reviewer

# Combine rubric and review into message.json
python3 -c "import json; rubric=json.load(open('output/rubric.json')); review=json.load(open('output/review.json')); json.dump({**rubric, **review}, open('output/message.json', 'w'))"

# Copy results back and upload to S3
cat output/message.json
aws s3 cp output/message.json ${OUTPUT_S3_URI}

# Rubric 1
Agent identifies that the CORS implementation uses a wildcard fallback and/or sticky static origin cache that violates CO-ORIGIN-ALLOW, +2
Agent updates the allowed_origins list to include both https://harbordesk.internal and https://ops.harbordesk.internal per G-2026-01, +3
Agent ensures Access-Control-Allow-Origin is only emitted for exact-match allowlisted origins and never emits the wildcard value, +3
Agent adds Vary: Origin header when an allowed origin grant is emitted, +2
Agent ensures preflight OPTIONS returns 204 with method/header/max-age hints only for allowed origins and no CORS headers for disallowed origins, +3
Agent sets preflight Access-Control-Max-Age to 300 instead of 600 per G-2026-02, +2
Agent removes in-process static caches from CORS/origin helpers so each request reflects current state independently, +2
Agent emits Access-Control-Allow-Credentials true only for allowed origins and never unconditionally, +1

# Rubric 2
Agent reorders bootstrap logic so already-bootstrapped check comes before secret validation per G-2026-05, +3
Agent changes the already-bootstrapped refusal HTTP status from 403 to 409 per G-2026-03, +3
Agent removes the allow_plaintext_bootstrap bypass so bootstrap always requires the correct X-Bootstrap-Secret header, +2
Agent stores the administrative token as a non-recoverable SHA-256 hash instead of the raw token per AC-TOKEN-STORE, +3
Agent sets the token file permissions to 0600 (owner read/write only) per AC-TOKEN-STORE, +2
Agent returns the raw token exactly once in the 201 bootstrap response body, +1
Agent uses constant-time comparison (hash_equals) for both bootstrap secret and token verification, +2
Agent removes in-process static caches for admin_token_exists so repeated bootstrap attempts within the same PHP process are correctly refused, +2

# Rubric 3
Agent changes the health endpoint missing-credential denial reason from missing_token to missing_credentials per G-2026-04, +3
Agent implements token verification by hashing the presented bearer token and comparing against the stored digest, +3
Agent disables debug mode (sets debug to false) so no X-Debug-Mode header or trace field is disclosed per EH-NO-DISCLOSE, +3
Agent ensures error responses use generic JSON body format with no stack trace or internal detail when debug is off, +2
Agent removes any in-process token read cache so health checks reflect current on-disk token state per request, +2
Agent preserves the invalid_token reason for present-but-wrong credentials which is unchanged by amendments, +1

# Rubric 4
Agent investigates the SQLite audit.db schema to identify the legacy layout with actor NOT NULL column and missing origin column causing insert failures, +2
Agent implements an idempotent runtime schema migration that runs on every database open rather than as a one-off deploy-time script, +3
Agent migration preserves all pre-existing historical rows in the ledger without data loss, +5
Agent migration adds an origin column so new audit rows record the request origin, +3
Agent migration handles the legacy actor column by removing the NOT NULL constraint or restructuring so inserts no longer fail, +2
Agent ensures audit_log inserts succeed after migration and record event, route, origin, decision, and reason for every audited request, +2
Agent ensures the start.sh script serves requests through the front controller with index.php as the router argument to php built-in server, +2
Agent verifies PHP syntax is valid after making code changes, +1

                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires hardening and debugging a PHP-based "HarborDesk Edge API"
to bring it into conformance with a security standard document (HEAS) and its
Appendix G amendments. The agent must fix CORS, authentication, bootstrap
ordering, audit-ledger schema migration, debug disclosure, and token storage
across several PHP files. The solution replaces config, http, audit, index, and
start scripts with corrected implementations. The test suite includes 15
deterministic tests plus a randomized property-based lifecycle test (45 cases)
driven by a hidden reference simulator in helpers.py.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Non-Canonical Docker Base Image
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 1)
Problem: The task uses the Python 3.13 slim-bookworm base image, but the
         primary languages are PHP, Bash, and SQL. Python is needed only for
         the test runner (pytest), not for the task itself. A PHP-specific or
         generic Debian canonical base image may be more appropriate, or this
         image should be justified as the closest canonical option for tasks
         that need both PHP (via apt) and Python (for pytest).

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FROM public.ecr.aws/docker/library/python:3.13-slim-bookworm@sha256:...   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The Python base provides pytest support natively, and PHP is
installed via apt. This is a pragmatic choice but may not match the canonical
base image list expectations for a PHP/security task. If the canonical list
includes this image as acceptable for tasks needing pytest, this is fine.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Filename Overlap Between Solution and Environment (Sub-check A)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/solution/ and tbench-task/environment/harbordesk/
Problem: Multiple files in the solution directory share basenames with files
         already present in the environment: config.php, index.php, audit.php,
         http.php, start.sh. While this is expected for a "fix the broken code"
         task (the environment contains the buggy versions and the solution
         contains the corrected versions), the overlap should be noted for
         the leakage check.

Current approach: The environment ships deliberately broken/misconfigured PHP
files. The solution replaces them with corrected versions via solve.sh's
`cp` commands.

Explanation: This is NOT leakage — the environment files are intentionally
buggy (debug enabled, wildcard CORS, wrong bootstrap ordering, stale static
caches, missing audit migration). The agent must derive fixes from the
standard document. The solution files contain meaningfully different logic.
No action required, but flagged for transparency.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Expert Time Estimate Seems High for Stated Difficulty
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 13)

Current approach: difficulty = "medium" with expert_time_estimate_min = 120
and junior_time_estimate_min = 240.

Rationale: A 2-hour expert estimate is more typical of "hard" tasks. The task
involves reading a lengthy standard document (~300KB), reconciling amendments,
fixing multiple interacting bugs across PHP files, implementing a schema
migration, and getting CORS/auth/audit exactly right. Consider either raising
difficulty to "hard" or justifying why "medium" is correct given the time
estimates.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted security/debugging task that realistically tests an
agent's ability to read policy documents, reconcile amendments, and implement
precise behavioral fixes across multiple PHP files under tight specification
constraints.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent test coverage with both deterministic and randomized property
    tests (45 lifecycle replays against a hidden reference simulator)
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating design: the reference simulator (helpers.py) is
    test-only; the agent must derive behavior from the standard document
  ✓ Realistic debug-and-harden scenario with intentionally planted bugs
    (stale caches, wrong ordering, missing migration, debug disclosure)

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Difficulty/time-estimate alignment could be reconsidered (120 min expert
    estimate is high for "medium")

Evaluates: Security policy interpretation, PHP debugging, database schema
           migration, CORS/authentication implementation

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  The task is structurally sound, well-tested, and follows Terminal-Bench 2.0
  conventions correctly. The single warning about base image choice is minor
  and pragmatically justified. No critical issues found.

output_data.review:
================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires hardening and debugging a PHP-based "HarborDesk Edge API"
to bring it into conformance with a security standard document (HEAS) and its
Appendix G amendments. The agent must fix CORS, authentication, bootstrap
ordering, audit-ledger schema migration, debug disclosure, and token storage
across several PHP files. The solution replaces config, http, audit, index, and
start scripts with corrected implementations. The test suite includes 15
deterministic tests plus a randomized property-based lifecycle test (45 cases)
driven by a hidden reference simulator in helpers.py.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Non-Canonical Docker Base Image
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 1)
Problem: The task uses the Python 3.13 slim-bookworm base image, but the
         primary languages are PHP, Bash, and SQL. Python is needed only for
         the test runner (pytest), not for the task itself. A PHP-specific or
         generic Debian canonical base image may be more appropriate, or this
         image should be justified as the closest canonical option for tasks
         that need both PHP (via apt) and Python (for pytest).

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FROM public.ecr.aws/docker/library/python:3.13-slim-bookworm@sha256:...   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The Python base provides pytest support natively, and PHP is
installed via apt. This is a pragmatic choice but may not match the canonical
base image list expectations for a PHP/security task. If the canonical list
includes this image as acceptable for tasks needing pytest, this is fine.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Filename Overlap Between Solution and Environment (Sub-check A)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/solution/ and tbench-task/environment/harbordesk/
Problem: Multiple files in the solution directory share basenames with files
         already present in the environment: config.php, index.php, audit.php,
         http.php, start.sh. While this is expected for a "fix the broken code"
         task (the environment contains the buggy versions and the solution
         contains the corrected versions), the overlap should be noted for
         the leakage check.

Current approach: The environment ships deliberately broken/misconfigured PHP
files. The solution replaces them with corrected versions via solve.sh's
`cp` commands.

Explanation: This is NOT leakage — the environment files are intentionally
buggy (debug enabled, wildcard CORS, wrong bootstrap ordering, stale static
caches, missing audit migration). The agent must derive fixes from the
standard document. The solution files contain meaningfully different logic.
No action required, but flagged for transparency.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Expert Time Estimate Seems High for Stated Difficulty
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 13)

Current approach: difficulty = "medium" with expert_time_estimate_min = 120
and junior_time_estimate_min = 240.

Rationale: A 2-hour expert estimate is more typical of "hard" tasks. The task
involves reading a lengthy standard document (~300KB), reconciling amendments,
fixing multiple interacting bugs across PHP files, implementing a schema
migration, and getting CORS/auth/audit exactly right. Consider either raising
difficulty to "hard" or justifying why "medium" is correct given the time
estimates.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted security/debugging task that realistically tests an
agent's ability to read policy documents, reconcile amendments, and implement
precise behavioral fixes across multiple PHP files under tight specification
constraints.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent test coverage with both deterministic and randomized property
    tests (45 lifecycle replays against a hidden reference simulator)
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating design: the reference simulator (helpers.py) is
    test-only; the agent must derive behavior from the standard document
  ✓ Realistic debug-and-harden scenario with intentionally planted bugs
    (stale caches, wrong ordering, missing migration, debug disclosure)

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Difficulty/time-estimate alignment could be reconsidered (120 min expert
    estimate is high for "medium")

Evaluates: Security policy interpretation, PHP debugging, database schema
           migration, CORS/authentication implementation

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  The task is structurally sound, well-tested, and follows Terminal-Bench 2.0
  conventions correctly. The single warning about base image choice is minor
  and pragmatically justified. No critical issues found.
================================================================================
output_data.rubric:
# Rubric 1
Agent identifies that the CORS implementation uses a wildcard fallback and/or sticky static origin cache that violates CO-ORIGIN-ALLOW, +2
Agent updates the allowed_origins list to include both https://harbordesk.internal and https://ops.harbordesk.internal per G-2026-01, +3
Agent ensures Access-Control-Allow-Origin is only emitted for exact-match allowlisted origins and never emits the wildcard value, +3
Agent adds Vary: Origin header when an allowed origin grant is emitted, +2
Agent ensures preflight OPTIONS returns 204 with method/header/max-age hints only for allowed origins and no CORS headers for disallowed origins, +3
Agent sets preflight Access-Control-Max-Age to 300 instead of 600 per G-2026-02, +2
Agent removes in-process static caches from CORS/origin helpers so each request reflects current state independently, +2
Agent emits Access-Control-Allow-Credentials true only for allowed origins and never unconditionally, +1

# Rubric 2
Agent reorders bootstrap logic so already-bootstrapped check comes before secret validation per G-2026-05, +3
Agent changes the already-bootstrapped refusal HTTP status from 403 to 409 per G-2026-03, +3
Agent removes the allow_plaintext_bootstrap bypass so bootstrap always requires the correct X-Bootstrap-Secret header, +2
Agent stores the administrative token as a non-recoverable SHA-256 hash instead of the raw token per AC-TOKEN-STORE, +3
Agent sets the token file permissions to 0600 (owner read/write only) per AC-TOKEN-STORE, +2
Agent returns the raw token exactly once in the 201 bootstrap response body, +1
Agent uses constant-time comparison (hash_equals) for both bootstrap secret and token verification, +2
Agent removes in-process static caches for admin_token_exists so repeated bootstrap attempts within the same PHP process are correctly refused, +2

# Rubric 3
Agent changes the health endpoint missing-credential denial reason from missing_token to missing_credentials per G-2026-04, +3
Agent implements token verification by hashing the presented bearer token and comparing against the stored digest, +3
Agent disables debug mode (sets debug to false) so no X-Debug-Mode header or trace field is disclosed per EH-NO-DISCLOSE, +3
Agent ensures error responses use generic JSON body format with no stack trace or internal detail when debug is off, +2
Agent removes any in-process token read cache so health checks reflect current on-disk token state per request, +2
Agent preserves the invalid_token reason for present-but-wrong credentials which is unchanged by amendments, +1

# Rubric 4
Agent investigates the SQLite audit.db schema to identify the legacy layout with actor NOT NULL column and missing origin column causing insert failures, +2
Agent implements an idempotent runtime schema migration that runs on every database open rather than as a one-off deploy-time script, +3
Agent migration preserves all pre-existing historical rows in the ledger without data loss, +5
Agent migration adds an origin column so new audit rows record the request origin, +3
Agent migration handles the legacy actor column by removing the NOT NULL constraint or restructuring so inserts no longer fail, +2
Agent ensures audit_log inserts succeed after migration and record event, route, origin, decision, and reason for every audited request, +2
Agent ensures the start.sh script serves requests through the front controller with index.php as the router argument to php built-in server, +2
Agent verifies PHP syntax is valid after making code changes, +1

[Container] 2026/06/27 13:13:23.334925 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found; task may use steps/*/instruction.md'
Our HarborDesk Edge API in `/app/harbordesk` still reflects an older edition of
the HarborDesk Edge API Hardening Standard in `/app/docs/standard.md`. Operators
report it disagrees with policy on several fronts, and the SQLite audit ledger at
`/app/harbordesk/data/audit.db` has stopped taking new rows even though requests
still get answers. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4).

Bring the running service into conformance so that, for every request, the API
returns exactly the status, headers, and body the Standard as amended requires
and records exactly the audit row it implies. Reconcile the full set of controls
the tests exercise: exact-origin CORS and preflight behavior, bootstrap secret
and already-bootstrapped ordering, AC-TOKEN-STORE token handling (the raw bearer
token is returned once but must not be persisted in recoverable form, and the
token file is owner-only), authenticated health checks and denial reasons,
debug disclosure rules, and audit logging.

Keep the existing routes (`GET /health`, `POST /admin/bootstrap`) and their JSON
shapes. The ledger already holds historical rows that reconciliation must
preserve; note that the on-disk ledger is restored to its older layout before
every run, so the migration has to be idempotent runtime code that reconciles
the schema whenever the database is opened (effectively on each request) — a
one-off migration done once at deploy time will be wiped and won't survive.
Verification replays many request lifecycles against one long-lived PHP process,
so behavior must be correct per request — cross-origin grants, bootstrap
eligibility, and credential checks must follow the current request and on-disk
state, not stale in-process bookkeeping left over from earlier requests in the
same run.

PHP, SQLite, and `curl` are already installed and everything runs offline.

Agent adds Vary: Origin header when an allowed origin grant is emitted, +2
Agent ensures preflight OPTIONS returns 204 with method/header/max-age hints only for allowed origins and no CORS headers for disallowed origins, +3
Agent sets preflight Access-Control-Max-Age to 300 instead of 600 per G-2026-02, +2
Agent removes in-process static caches from CORS/origin helpers so each request reflects current state independently, +2
Agent emits Access-Control-Allow-Credentials true only for allowed origins and never unconditionally, +1

# Rubric 2
Agent reorders bootstrap logic so already-bootstrapped check comes before secret validation per G-2026-05, +3
Agent changes the already-bootstrapped refusal HTTP status from 403 to 409 per G-2026-03, +3
Agent removes the allow_plaintext_bootstrap bypass so bootstrap always requires the correct X-Bootstrap-Secret header, +2
Agent stores the administrative token as a non-recoverable SHA-256 hash instead of the raw token per AC-TOKEN-STORE, +3
Agent sets the token file permissions to 0600 (owner read/write only) per AC-TOKEN-STORE, +2
Agent returns the raw token exactly once in the 201 bootstrap response body, +1
Agent uses constant-time comparison (hash_equals) for both bootstrap secret and token verification, +2
Agent removes in-process static caches for admin_token_exists so repeated bootstrap attempts within the same PHP process are correctly refused, +2

Agent implements token verification by hashing the presented bearer token and comparing against the stored digest, +3

Agent ensures error responses use generic JSON body format with no stack trace or internal detail when debug is off, +2

                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires hardening and debugging a PHP-based "HarborDesk Edge API"
to bring it into conformance with a security standard document (HEAS) and its
Appendix G amendments. The agent must fix CORS, authentication, bootstrap
ordering, audit-ledger schema migration, debug disclosure, and token storage
across several PHP files. The solution replaces config, http, audit, index, and
start scripts with corrected implementations. The test suite includes 15
deterministic tests plus a randomized property-based lifecycle test (45 cases)
driven by a hidden reference simulator in helpers.py.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Non-Canonical Docker Base Image
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 1)
Problem: The task uses the Python 3.13 slim-bookworm base image, but the
         primary languages are PHP, Bash, and SQL. Python is needed only for
         the test runner (pytest), not for the task itself. A PHP-specific or
         generic Debian canonical base image may be more appropriate, or this
         image should be justified as the closest canonical option for tasks
         that need both PHP (via apt) and Python (for pytest).

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FROM public.ecr.aws/docker/library/python:3.13-slim-bookworm@sha256:...   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The Python base provides pytest support natively, and PHP is
installed via apt. This is a pragmatic choice but may not match the canonical
base image list expectations for a PHP/security task. If the canonical list
includes this image as acceptable for tasks needing pytest, this is fine.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Filename Overlap Between Solution and Environment (Sub-check A)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/solution/ and tbench-task/environment/harbordesk/
Problem: Multiple files in the solution directory share basenames with files
         already present in the environment: config.php, index.php, audit.php,
         http.php, start.sh. While this is expected for a "fix the broken code"
         task (the environment contains the buggy versions and the solution
         contains the corrected versions), the overlap should be noted for
         the leakage check.

Current approach: The environment ships deliberately broken/misconfigured PHP
files. The solution replaces them with corrected versions via solve.sh's
`cp` commands.

Explanation: This is NOT leakage — the environment files are intentionally
buggy (debug enabled, wildcard CORS, wrong bootstrap ordering, stale static
caches, missing audit migration). The agent must derive fixes from the
standard document. The solution files contain meaningfully different logic.
No action required, but flagged for transparency.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Expert Time Estimate Seems High for Stated Difficulty
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 13)

Current approach: difficulty = "medium" with expert_time_estimate_min = 120
and junior_time_estimate_min = 240.

Rationale: A 2-hour expert estimate is more typical of "hard" tasks. The task
involves reading a lengthy standard document (~300KB), reconciling amendments,
fixing multiple interacting bugs across PHP files, implementing a schema
migration, and getting CORS/auth/audit exactly right. Consider either raising
difficulty to "hard" or justifying why "medium" is correct given the time
estimates.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted security/debugging task that realistically tests an
agent's ability to read policy documents, reconcile amendments, and implement
precise behavioral fixes across multiple PHP files under tight specification
constraints.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent test coverage with both deterministic and randomized property
    tests (45 lifecycle replays against a hidden reference simulator)
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating design: the reference simulator (helpers.py) is
    test-only; the agent must derive behavior from the standard document
  ✓ Realistic debug-and-harden scenario with intentionally planted bugs
    (stale caches, wrong ordering, missing migration, debug disclosure)

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Difficulty/time-estimate alignment could be reconsidered (120 min expert
    estimate is high for "medium")

Evaluates: Security policy interpretation, PHP debugging, database schema
           migration, CORS/authentication implementation

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  The task is structurally sound, well-tested, and follows Terminal-Bench 2.0
  conventions correctly. The single warning about base image choice is minor
  and pragmatically justified. No critical issues found.

fields.label: On a scale form 1 to 5, how good is the last review?
fields.description:
1- spam

2- bad review (small mistakes that make the review bad)

3- Okay- A good review but maybe it there were some things that were not identified in the first reviews

4- Really good review - Nice feedback, all comments are accurate and provided a clear way to fix the task. The reviewer also identified some issues that were not spotted by our evals

5- Stellar review - The submitter should've been able to fix the task based on the comments
